main_sections.title: Twin Melodies
main_sections.drama_summary: Twin Melodies is a three-act play by Mitra Phukan set in contemporary India that explores the tension between classical musical tradition and modern Indo-Western fusion. Act I opens in Iqbal's room, where a youth fusion group — Shruti on violin, Avinash on tabla, Iqbal on flute, and Peter on keyboard — wraps up a rehearsal of an Indo-Western fusion piece. When Iqbal reads aloud a newspaper review praising Shruti's violin performance with her father, the mood turns celebratory for the boys but reveals Shruti's distress. She has been secretly attending fusion practice sessions without her father's knowledge, fearing he will regard playing the violin to Western tunes as a desecration of classical Hindustani music. Encouraged by her friends to 'bite the bullet', she resolves to raise the matter at dinner. Act II moves to the Sharma dining room after dinner. Shruti, still lost in thought over her uneaten dosa, is gently teased by both parents before she finally musters the courage to tell her father, Nabin, about the upcoming Indo-Western fusion concert. Nabin reacts with cold, firm disapproval — citing his rule of one performance in six months, his contempt for fusion music, and his concern that Shruti is drowning her individual style in an orchestra. Despite Shruti's plea to attend a single rehearsal before deciding, Nabin dismisses the idea with mockery and leaves the table. Leela, Shruti's mother, scolds her daughter for hiding the practice sessions; Shruti apologises and promises never to hide again. Act III returns to Iqbal's house. Shruti privately considers practising one last time and then withdrawing from the concert. Mid-rehearsal, Nabin and Leela arrive unannounced and stand in a corner. The music draws them in; Nabin is soon seen tapping his feet and the act ends with him clapping furiously, his face alight with pride. He praises Shruti specifically for never losing sight of the raga notes, then offers the children his music room for regular practice. In a quiet aside, Leela reveals to Shruti that Nabin himself had once defied his own family — a traditional vocalist household — to take up the violin, a Western instrument, and had worked his fingers to the bone to succeed. This parallel history softens Nabin's earlier rigidity and prompts his moving admission: 'I underestimated the power of our own music. I was afraid you would be lost to us.' Recognising each artist's unique path — 'each bay, its own wind' — he declares his trust in Shruti and his support at the concert. The play's central thematic conflict, tradition versus modernity, is ultimately resolved through intergenerational empathy and music's own power to transcend ideological boundaries.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Reflect and Respond + Reading for Meaning + Check Your Understanding + Critical Reflection

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Given below are some stringed musical instruments. Work in pairs and identify them by first letter of the spellings of their names.
Do you play a musical instrument? If given a choice, which one would you choose and why?
Is there any difference in the choice of music between children and elders? If yes, why? If no, why not?

Work in pairs to complete the paragraph given on the next page by choosing the correct phrases given in the box. Discuss with your teacher whether the following phrases have a literal meaning or a figurative one.

question_bank.stem: The theatre club was 1. _______________________ their practice.
Anil saw Sunny was 2. _______________________ and not speaking to anyone.
They had a disagreement in the morning and since then, Sunny was 3. _______________________.
Anil did not want to 4. _______________________ and disturb Sunny further but he finally decided to 5. _______________________ and speak to Sunny.
He was sure if he apologised first, his friend would 6. _______________________.
With a lot of anxiety, he 7. _______________________ to apologise.
Everyone clapped and asked them to 8. _______________________ to celebrate.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Do you think Shruti will gather the courage to speak to her father? Give a reason.
What might be Shruti's father's reaction? Why?

Complete the summary of Act II by choosing the correct words from the box given below. There are two extra words that you do not need. Check your answers with the teacher. (Box: disapproval, plea, absent-minded, setback, surprised, scolded, courage, firm, clash, relief)

question_bank.stem: Shruti seemed 1. _______________________ during dinner.
She finally gathered the 2. _______________________ to inform her father.
Nabin reacted negatively, expressing 3. _______________________ of fusion music.
Despite Shruti's 4. _______________________ to him to attend a practice before deciding, Nabin was 5. _______________________ in his decision.
Shruti's mother, Leela, 6. _______________________ her for not seeking permission earlier.
The tension highlights the 7. _______________________ between traditional and contemporary musical preferences.
Despite the 8. _______________________, Shruti promises not to hide again.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Will Shruti's father go for the rehearsal? Support your answer with a reason.
Do you think Shruti and her parents would understand one another? If yes, why? If no, why not?
Work in pairs. Identify the true statements. Check the answers with your teacher.

question_bank.stem: During the rehearsal, Shruti's parents enter the room and are overjoyed at her performance.
Shruti initially considers telling her friends that she can't join them for the performance, after practising one last time.
Shruti's father expressed that she had sung a few notes incorrectly during her performance.
Shruti's father had followed the family's tradition of being a violinist and worked very hard to achieve all that he did.
Shruti's mother helped her husband realise why he should support their daughter.
Shruti's father accepts that he underrated the potential of classical music.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the extracts given below and answer the questions that follow.

question_bank.stem: Extract 1 — Shruti: There is need to worry Iqbal! … I just don't know how to tell him in the right way! / Iqbal: All ways are right ways, Shruti. He is your father, he will understand. (i) Select the option that correctly identifies Shruti's situation: A. confident B. indifferent C. content D. in a dilemma
Extract 1 (ii) What does Shruti mean by 'the right way', in the sentence, 'I just don't know how to tell him in the right way!'?
Extract 1 (iii) Complete the following with a suitable reason. We can say that the extract reflects the generational gap in understanding art and tradition because ____________.
Extract 1 (iv) Explain how Iqbal's words were meant as an assurance to Shruti.
Extract 1 (v) Fill in the blank with a phrase from the extract. The critics argued that only films which provoke thoughtful discussion are _________.

Extract 2 — Nabin: I underestimated the power of our own music … I trust you Shruti and I will root for your group at the concert! (i) Select the suitable option to complete the sentence. Nabin's breakthrough in his understanding of artistic freedom, highlights a bridge between ______. A. personal ambition and professional success B. traditional values and modern expressions C. strict discipline and casual learning D. historical techniques and futuristic innovations
Extract 2 (ii) Fill in the blank with a suitable phrase. The extract uses the word 'lost' both in the sense of losing _________ and physical separation.
Extract 2 (iii) In the line, 'Shruti hugs her parents', which word from those given below does not show what it conveys? reconciliation / harmony / duty / acceptance / understanding
Extract 2 (iv) What can be inferred about Shruti's future in music following her father's expression of trust and support?
Extract 2 (v) Identify the phrase that refers to the uniqueness of each artist's journey and style.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Justify the title 'Twin Melodies'. How does the play explore the theme of tradition and modernity?

What are the different perspectives of Peter, Iqbal, and Avinash regarding confronting figures of authority? How do their attitudes towards Shruti's dilemma with her father provide insight into their own characters and values?
Analyse the character of Nabin Sharma. How does his attitude towards music evolve throughout the play?
How does the play use Shruti's internal conflict to explore the theme of duty towards family versus personal passion?
Evaluate the effectiveness of the play's conclusion. Does it realistically address the conflicts presented?
Assess how well the role of cultural diversity contributes to the storyline of the play.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the highlighted word given in the brackets. SHRUTI (Aside): Oh, how much I enjoy performing this piece… What is 'aside' used for? (i) enables characters to talk about the story (ii) shows what a character thinks or feels (iii) makes a direct link between the audience and the character (iv) (i), (ii), and (iii)

question_bank.stem: What is 'aside' used for?
options: enables characters to talk about the story
shows what a character thinks or feels
makes a direct link between the audience and the character

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the following words from the text: Ahem ahem, I…er… Why are they used? 1. to fill gaps while speaking 2. to increase clarity 3. to provide confidence. Now, choose suitable non-lexical fillers given in the box (whew, err, arrgh, hmmm, ahem-ahem) to describe the emoticons 1 to 4. There is an extra word you do not need.

question_bank.stem: Why are non-lexical fillers used?
Match each of the four emoticons to the correct non-lexical filler from: whew, err, arrgh, hmmm, ahem-ahem (one is extra).

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Match the meanings of music-related words in Column 1 with their meanings in Column 2.

question_bank.stem: Match: 1. rhythm 2. tempo 3. bass 4. baritone 5. cacophony 6. pitch 7. scale — with meanings (i)–(vii).
question_bank.table:
Column1|Column2
rhythm|(iii) the pattern of beats or time intervals in music
tempo|(iv) the speed or pace at which a piece of music is played
bass|(i) the lowest tone or part in music
baritone|(v) a male voice that falls between the higher and lower ranges
cacophony|(ii) a mixture of loud unpleasant sounds
pitch|(vii) the perceived frequency of a sound, how high or low a sound is
scale|(vi) a series of musical notes moving upwards or downwards

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Write the meanings of the underlined phrases based on your understanding of the usage in sentences: 1. drown your individual style 2. sense of musicality 3. go down the drain.

question_bank.stem: Write the meaning of the phrase 'drown your individual style' as used in: 'When you follow trends without thinking, you drown your individual style.'
Write the meaning of the phrase 'sense of musicality' as used in: 'The pianist was appreciated for his sense of musicality.'
Write the meaning of the phrase 'go down the drain' as used in: 'When one of the performers in the group was injured, they felt their practise would go down the drain.'

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the dialogues: 1. Oh, how much I enjoy performing this piece! 2. Wah, how soulful! You all have done a wonderful job! Sentences 1 and 2 are ___________ sentences. (declarative/interrogative/exclamatory/imperative). Now, convert the exclamatory sentences to declarative sentences. Use 'exclaimed' or 'exclaimed with (feeling)' instead of reporting verb 'said'. Remove interjections, exclamatory mark, and quotations.

question_bank.stem: Identify the sentence type: 'Oh, how much I enjoy performing this piece!' and 'Wah, how soulful! You all have done a wonderful job!'
Convert the two exclamatory sentences to declarative sentences using 'exclaimed' or 'exclaimed with (feeling)'.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Write the sentences given below in reported speech. One example has been done for you.

question_bank.stem: 1.“Wow Shruti! You’re a sensation!” suggested Avinash. Avinash exclaimed with wonder that Shruti was a great sensation.
2. 'What an incredible achievement! I can't believe we pulled it off!' said Iqbal. → Iqbal exclaimed that it _____________________________.
3. 'Bravo! Each note was executed perfectly, and the performance was flawless!' said Shruti's mother. → Shruti's mother applauded that ____________________.
4. Iqbal: I basically have a little bit of scolding for breakfast every day! (convert to reported speech)
5. Shruti: Sorry mummy, won't hide from you again. I promise! (convert to reported speech)
6. Avinash: You are a sensation now! The least you can do is throw a party! (convert to reported speech)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Report the following dialogue in the indirect form of speech. (Asma/Deepa conversation about spilling paint at the art studio)

question_bank.stem: Asma exclaimed excitedly that she 1.________________________. Deepa urged her to tell her as she was eager to hear. Asma said that 2. ______. Deepa pitied Asma. However, Asma replied to her not to worry as 3. ________. Deepa exclaimed that 4. ____________.

listening.transcript_text: A musician introduces the yazh (yāḻ), describing it as an Indian harp used in ancient Tamil music. It is referred to as the ancestor of the modern-day veena, named so because the tip of the instrument's stem was carved into the head of the mythological animal Yali. The yazh was an open-stringed instrument featuring gut strings, a wooden boat-shaped skin-covered resonator, and an ebony stem. It was tuned by either pegs or rings of gut moved up and down the strings. Depending on design variations, the instrument took different names: 'mayuri yazh' (peacock yazh), 'makara yazh' (crocodile yazh), 'matsya yazh' (fish yazh), and 'vil yazh' (bow string yazh). The speaker encourages the audience to visualise the instrument from these descriptions and expresses hope that they have enjoyed learning about the yazh.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Look at the given stringed musical instruments and decide which one could be the yazh [yāḻ].
Now, listen to a musician describing the yazh and check if your choice was correct. (Transcript for teacher on page 270)
Listen to the musician's description of the yazh once again. As you listen, complete the notes below with not more than three words.

question_bank.stem: 1. Type of instrument – The Indian _______ (i) Was used in: __________ (ii) Earlier version of: __________
2. Design (i) stem tip – shaped like _____ of mythological animal (ii) resonator A. shape of a _________ B. material – _________
3. Design variety (i) peacock, _________, _________, and bow string

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Stress and intonation exercises: Let us pick a few lines from the play and speak them aloud. Focus on how stress and intonation can change their meaning or emotional impact. (Lines provided with stress and intonation guidance: 'I don't want papa to feel that I don't care about his opinions, because I do.'; 'Tonight, at dinner, I promise to bring this up.'; 'Why are you lost in thought my dear child?'; 'Each bay, its own wind')

question_bank.stem: Perform the given lines with the indicated stress and intonation patterns. Discuss how the stress and intonation change the emotional impact.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Practise speaking aloud a few other sentences from the play on your own. Understanding what is being said and why will help you stress the suitable portions and use the appropriate tone.

Read the beginning of a story given below. (Anuradha, a teenager, extraordinary tabla player; father a vocalist who disapproves; she decides to perform at a prestigious event despite his disapproval; mother and brother support her.) In groups of four, create a play script based only on the beginning of the story given above. Tips: write rough draft; mention setting and characters; use colon after speaker; use brackets for stage directions; include 'aside' where needed; use non-lexical fillers; use ellipsis for pauses.

Study the words given in brackets in the following dialogues taken from the text. Answer: 1. Why do we not use inverted commas to convey the dialogues? 2. Where do we use colons? 3. What is written within the brackets? What do these convey? 4. How does it help the actors?

question_bank.stem: Why do we not use inverted commas to convey the dialogues in a play script?
Where do we use colons in a play script?
What is written within the brackets in stage directions? What do these convey?
How do stage directions in brackets help the actors?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Did you know that there is an orchestra that made musical instruments from recycling junk or discarded waste items? There is one such orchestra, called the Recycled Orchestra of Cateura, Paraguay, that was formed in 2012, and children are a part of it. They play musical instruments made from scrap materials collected from landfills. (Read and engage with this information — no specific exercise questions are set.)

Indigenous musical instruments are crafted from natural materials like bamboo, gourds, animal hides, and shells, reflecting a deep connection to the environment and cultural heritage. Find out more about such indigenous instruments that are used in your region.
In groups of five, select objects around yourself to create a rhythm. Present it in the class. Enjoy!
Read the book Rigmarole and Other Plays by Sai Paranjpye and enjoy the humour and wit.
